bibliographic_data.title: Evolving Interactive Narrative Worlds
bibliographic_data.abstract: An interactive narrative is bound by the context of the world where its story takes place. However, most work in interactive narrative generation takes its story world design and mechanics as given, which abdicates a large part of story generation to an external world designer. In this paper, we close the story world design gap with an evolutionary search framework for generating interactive narrative worlds and mechanics. Our framework finds story world designs that accommodate multiple distinct player roles. We evaluate our system with an action agreement ratio analysis that shows worlds generated by our framework provide a greater number of in-role action opportunities compared to story worlds randomly sampled from the generative space.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents an evolutionary search framework for generating interactive narrative worlds and mechanics that accommodate multiple distinct player roles, addressing a gap in current approaches by integrating concepts from procedural content generation (PCG) with experience management.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use an evolutionary search framework guided by an evaluation function to maximize the utility of distinct artificial player personas. The system generates PDDL-based narrative world designs and evaluates them using an Action Agreement Ratio (AAR) metric.
outcomes: Generated interactive narrative worlds that better accommodate different play styles compared to randomly sampled story worlds.
Provided a greater number of in-role action opportunities for players.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the gap between procedural content generation (PCG) and experience management by introducing an evolutionary search framework. This framework generates interactive narrative world designs that accommodate multiple distinct player roles, using PDDL as the design specification language. The system evaluates these designs through an Action Agreement Ratio (AAR) metric to ensure they provide a diverse range of in-role action opportunities for players.
